title: Letters to the Editor
summary: STATE TO POISON PERCH ALARMED FISH AND GAME GOES AFTER SALMON PREDATOR By Jon Little (Published June 8, 2000) Yellow perch look cute. They're small and lack the fangs of northern pike. But, like pike, perch are invasive predators. And they're scaring the tar out of state fisheries biologists. Perch aren't native to Alaska and, once introduced, could easily devastate salmon-producing streams by gorging themselves on eggs and fingerlings, say biologists who six weeks ago discovered a lake full of the harmless-looking fish in Nikiski. "We don't have to amplify our concern on this one," said Mike Bethe, area management biologist. "On a one to 10 scale, it's a 10." The state plans to poison the unnamed, private lake a little later this summer, hoping to wipe out the perch that have devoured jus…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | OFFICIALS URGE AID FOR ASI SEAFOOD PLANT ASKS FOR $15 MILLION MORE By Wesley Loy Daily News Reporter (Published August 15, 2000) A state lending agency is being deluged by letters from lawmakers and business people urging it to provide $15 million more in financing to keep a struggling new seafood factory running in Anchorage. State Rep. Gail Phillips, R-Homer, urged the Alaska Industrial Development and Export Authority not to "turn its back" on the Alaska Seafood International plant, whose construction and operation have already consumed more than $50 million in state money. "The naysayers would like for you to do nothing and sit back and say, 'I told you so!' However, too many people and too much private and public money have been invested in this plant for us to just throw our ha…

title: EAT PIKE AND SAVE SALMON
summary: "Pike are delicious," author Jim Morrow says in "The Freshwater Fishes of Alaska." "The meat is white, flaky and flavorful. The so-called muddy taste of which some people complain is confined to the skin." If pike are scaled, scrubbed and washed, the muddy taste is eliminated. Usually, skinning them is the easiest solution. "Northern Cookbook" offers this recipe: Get a fresh, dressed -- but unscaled -- fish. Wash it and pat it damp-dry. Sprinkle the flesh with salt and monosodium glutamate. Place slices of onion inside the fish. Combine butter, lemon juice and tarragon and sprinkle a tablespoon of this sauce on the onion. Close the fish with skewers and measure it at its thickest point to determine the cooking time. Wrap the fish in heavy brown paper and place it in a greased baking dish. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | CASHING IN ON WIND POWER VILLAGE OF WALES ERECTS WINDMILLS TO CUT RELIANCE ON DIESEL FUEL By Tony Hopfinger Daily News Reporter (Published August 13, 2000) Wales -- Summer at this beach village on the Bering Strait is marked by sandblasting winds and paint-chipped houses. Wind pelts people on four-wheelers. It thrusts sand deep between window panes. And when it blows really hard, it rips siding off houses and sends the aluminum scraps fluttering over the village like confetti in a parade. Workers position one of the new 80-foot wind towers in Wales. It and another windmill should begin generating electricity for the village this fall. (Tony Hopfinger / Anchorage Daily News) But the days of wind whisking carefree across Wales are ending. It is time for the wind to give back to the Inu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | AQUACULTURE TAKING ITS TOLL RESEARCHERS: FISH FARMING DOESN'T REDUCE TROUBLE FOR OCEANS FISH TOLL ON OCEANS By Rick Callahan The Associated Press (Published June 29, 2000) Commercial fish farms have long been touted as a way to boost the global seafood harvest while taking the pressure off Earth's overtaxed oceans. But it hasn't worked out that way, researchers say. They conclude that aquaculture - the production of everything from catfish to shrimp - has instead raised demand for some ocean fish such as anchovies that are ground into fish meal to fatten their domesticated cousins. What's more, the researchers say, fish farming has created vast amounts of animal waste that has fouled coastal areas where fish and shellfish are being raised. In addition, they say, domesticated fish suc…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 1,000 FIREFIGHTERS BATTLE BITTERROOT By Holly M. Sanders Daily News Reporter (Published August 10, 2000) With the heavy fire season over in Alaska, nearly 1,000 firefighters from around the state have headed south to help exhausted crews battle wildfires raging in the Lower 48. Most Alaskans have spent the past two weeks chasing a major blaze in southwest Montana. The 125,000-acre fire blew out of control Monday, forcing hundreds to evacuate and scorching dozens of homes. The Alaska Type 1 team -- one of two elite groups of firefighting managers along the Idaho-Montana border -- was slowly getting a grip on the 450-acre fire in the scenic Bitterroot Valley, when lightning storms created new blazes that stretched crews to the limit. Earlier this week, the blaze surrounded the firefigh…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | 'IT CERTAINLY LOOKS LIKE A POLAR BEAR' EXPERTS PUZZLE OVER SIGHTING IN JUNE NEAR WHITTIER By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published August 7, 2000) Texas tourist Betty Wasicek thought getting a picture of a polar bear was doing pretty good on her first tour of Alaska earlier this summer. "Oh, it was very exciting," Wasicek said last week from her home in Sugar Land, outside Houston. A Texas tourist took this photograph in Prince William Sound on June 19 of what appears to be a polar bear. (Photo by Betty Wasicek) Wait a minute. Back up. A picture of what again? "Oh, it's a polar bear." And you were where? "Oh, just right out of Whittier. Barry Glacier. Now, what day was that? Oh, yes, June 19." A polar bear. Earlier this summer. In Prince William Sound. "I know, it is kind of …

title: THE FUEL-CELL SOLUTION
summary: Placid amid the noise and haste of Alaska's largest airport is the quiet future of electric generation. Behind the U.S. Postal Service's airport facility, five solemn fuel cells hum a tune of clean, efficient, reliable energy. For many in the crowd that will gather there Wednesday at a ribbon cutting to open the facility, it is the gospel of the electrical future. The Anchorage post office effort may be the largest commercial fuel cell project in the world, according to the fuel-cell manufacturer. Last year, the Postal Service partnered with Anchorage electric utility Chugach Electric Association and a handful of government agencies and electric research groups to install the fuel cells. The cells produce about 1 million watts of electricity. That's more than enough to power the post offic…

summary: MONDAY The Anchorage Chamber of Commerce will meet at noon Monday in the 4th Avenue Theater. The theme is "Property Tax Cap Initiative Series -- Part One of Three." The issue will be introduced with its legal ramifications. The speakers are Lt. Gov. Fran Ulmer and Cheryl Frasca, director of the municipal budget office. Call 272-2401. The Architecture/Engineering Marketing Association of Alaska will meet at noon Monday in the Regal Alaskan Hotel. Lisa Sutherland, staff member for U.S. Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, will speak on "The Status of Alaska's Senate Appropriations." Call Maureen at 561-0790. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | BLAZE DESTROYS APARTMENTS MAN HELPS FAMILY FLEE 2ND FLOOR By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published August 14, 2000) Fire destroyed an East Anchorage four-plex Sunday afternoon and forced a family to make a second-story balcony escape as their apartment filled with smoke. The Settjes, in their second-floor apartment, were the only ones home at 6100 E. 41st Court when the fire started about 4 p.m. And Debra Settje said they didn't know their building was ablaze until one of her children opened the apartment door. "We opened the door, and the flames and smoke were right there," said Debra Settje, standing in the street in a steady rain with her son Stephen, 10, hugging her. Two other daughters, Hannah, 13, and Michelle, 11, and Settje's sister, Monique Seybold, were also in the …

summary: Monday, August 14, 2000 Europa Bakery owner Nicholas Kazan stands by two of the new ovens at his expanded facility in Midtown. The bakery can handle 15,000 pounds of dough daily in the 6,000 square-foot facility. (Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Check stock prices Weekly fuel prices survey LOAFING WITH A TWIST (Published: August 14, 2000) "Bread is the staff of life. We must nurture it." WORKER SETTING A TRAP? (Published: August 14, 2000) Question: We strongly suspect one of our new employees is a liar and a thief. Six weeks ago we began realizing she told small lies every time she made work errors. Why didn't we fire her?
